Threat.value: Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.

Negative Appraisals.value: I have permanently changed for the worse
I can't rely on other people
I have to be on guard all the time

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I work hard at keeping busy with other things

issues.explanation: Drug use is highly implausible for a 76-year-old Middle Eastern widowed female with accident trauma, especially without any supporting context in other items. This demographic has very low base rates for illicit drug use, and no other items suggest substance-related coping.

issues.item: Male Presence: Being alone with men
issues.explanation: Trigger related to being alone with men is inconsistent with an accident trauma type. Accident traumas typically involve situational triggers (vehicles, locations, sounds) rather than gender-specific interpersonal triggers, unless the accident involved male perpetrators which is not indicated.

issues.item: Risky Behavior: Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
issues.explanation: Risky behavior is clinically unexpected for a 76-year-old female with accident trauma. This age group and demographic typically shows avoidance and caution rather than risk-taking after accidents. No other items support a pattern of impulsivity or risk-seeking.

issues.item: Olfactory Recall: When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
issues.explanation: Strong olfactory recall is unusual for accident trauma unless it involved distinctive smells (burning, chemicals). Without context suggesting such smells in the accident, this sensory detail seems mismatched with typical accident memories which are more visual/auditory.

vignette:
Mrs. A, a 76-year-old widowed woman of Middle Eastern descent, presents with significant distress following a serious car accident two years ago. Her life is now dominated by a pervasive sense of threat. She is hypervigilant, startling easily at the sound of passing cars, and struggles with persistent insomnia. These feelings of danger are directly ignited by specific triggers: the sudden, unexpected touch of a passerby or the simple awareness of being observed can instantly flood her system with alarm.

The accident itself intrudes upon her constantly. Vivid visual memories replay like a film in her mind, accompanied by the auditory recall of screeching tires and crushing metal. These involuntary recollections are not just distressing; they powerfully reinforce her core negative beliefs. She feels permanently damaged by the event and is convinced she can no longer rely on others for safety. Consequently, she believes she must remain on guard at all times.

This combination of intrusive memory and negative appraisal creates a self-perpetuating cycle. The more the memories replay, the more confirmed she feels in her beliefs of permanent damage and the need for hypervigilance. In turn, these appraisals intensify her overall sense of threat and make the memories feel more immediate and dangerous. Her sleep suffers further, and her startle response becomes more pronounced.

To manage this overwhelming internal state, Mrs. A has adopted rigid coping strategies. She fills every waking moment with busyness to crowd out the thoughts. She meticulously avoids the site of the accident and any medical cues, such as hospitals or ambulances. These avoidant behaviors are a direct effort to reduce the feelings of threat and escape the painful memories, yet they prevent her from processing the trauma or challenging her beliefs. Isolated and weary, she remains trapped in a state of high alert, her world having shrunk to a narrow corridor of attempted safety.
